introduction: Localized physical manifestation of changes in blood supply to the skin, usually manifesting over bony prominences. It results from pressure or pressure in combination with friction/shear and may occur in institutional or home settings. It is a useful indicator of quality of care.

features: Stage I: Intact skin; non-blanchable erythema
Stage II: Ulcer extends through epidermis; no slough
Stage III: Full thickness loss of tissue; subcutaneous fat may be visible
Stage IV: Full thickness tissue loss; slough present, may extend to muscle, fascia, joint capsule
Unstageable/Unclassified: Full thickness tissue loss, depth obscured by slough and/or eschar in the wound bed
Suspected deep tissue injury: Localized area of discolored skin, initial boggy swelling
Erythema around edges of wound; enlarging wound
Foul odour; friable granulation tissue; increased necrotic tissue
Purulent exudate; tunneling; warmth; wound breakdown

investigations: Levine's technique for obtaining wound swabs

non_drug: Clean wound using 0.9% saline or lactated Ringer's solution
Adjunct treatment to facilitate wound healing: Electrical therapy, Electromagnetic therapy, Laser therapy, Ultrasound therapy, Vacuum-assisted closure
Prevent further injury: Avoid pressure on wound, Chair cushions for sitting, Keep heels and other pressure points off pressure, Pressure-reducing mattresses, Reposition every 2 hours
Support wound healing process: Adequate protein intake, Adequate calorie intake, Avoid exposure to cold, Correct deficiencies e.g. of vitamin C and zinc
drug: Analgesia: give appropriate pain medicines 30 minutes before wound procedures
Antibiotics: Topical and systemic antibiotics detailed for different conditions and bacteria
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Vancomycin may be oto- and nephro-toxic; may also cause blood dyscrasias and other complications. Its use should be restricted to cases of MRSA.

prevention: Address modifiable risk factors e.g. dry skin, immobility, nutritional factors, etc.
Incidence can be reduced up to 30% with aggressive prevention/intervention strategies
A multi-disciplinary wound team is an asset
Pressure relief: Improve mobility, Frequent turning, Pressure-relieving devices

Nutritional Interventions: Increase daily caloric intake for malnourished patients, Achieve daily protein intake, No routine supplementation with vitamin C or zinc unless deficiency is demonstrated, High serum zinc levels may interfere with healing
